speaker: interviewer
text: We are interviewing Phyllis Cooper Young. Today's date is November 6, 1995. Phyllis, you left Poland. How did you cross the border?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't easy because it was really rough. We went by the way of Czechoslovakia, again, stopped in Prague. Took us a long time to get there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know, about five or six.

speaker: interviewer
text: Girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, all girls, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you use for money, or how did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't even remember. I honest to God I can't remember how. I know we did have it. I remember going into a store in Prague and buying some. I don't know. I really don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you get any of your belongings back from your apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you go in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came into Munich. And from there we were sent to Feldafing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who sent you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember how it happened. We must have gone into some kind of Jewish organization, committee, or maybe one of the Americans. Because that was-- by then, I think it was August already. And uh--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were in the American zone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know that you were going to the American zone?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I-- I don't even think at that time that I knew that there was an American, a British, or French or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long did you --

speaker: interviewee
text: But I knew that, you know, you talk to people. I knew they were saying that all the action is around there, that the Jewish organizations are sending food, money, clothes, which is true.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you want to leave Europe at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Did I want to leave Europe? Well, let me tell you something. I always wanted to go to America. Even as a kid, I  used to adore American movies. I used to adore-- we had them with the Polish subtitles, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: What movies did you remember seeing as a young--

speaker: interviewee
text: All the Westerns. I remember Tom Mix. I remember-- oh, I was getting very excited about Wind, but that never came because  the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you never saw it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I read the book. I was a reader.

speaker: interviewer
text: You read the book?

speaker: interviewee
text: I read the book.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was very, very much into American movies.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your goal to go to America then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not a goal, a dream.

speaker: interviewer
text: A dream, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hollywood. A dream, not a goal.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in Feldafing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I told you I got into Feldafing around August. And then in October I went to this place, this American hospital, the APO.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the name of this hospital? Where was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bad Wiessee.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll kill myself. B-A-D W-I-E-S-S-E-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of a hospital was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a APO. I didn't know what APO was then.

speaker: interviewer
text: What it is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was for American soldiers who were wounded who were not well enough to be shipped to the States yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a recuperation area?

speaker: interviewee
text: For American soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your duties in this hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were showing a movie every night. After the movies, me and another girl, we gave them doughnuts, coffee. We helped out in the PX when they came for cigarettes or toothpaste or toothbrush or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Who was with you in this hospital of your friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eight guys. We were-- there were eight guys and two girls. And one of the men was my future husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you meet him?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Feldafing.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Feldafing.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was the one that came to me and asked me if I wanted to get out. And even though we were free, it was still sleeping on bunk beds, standing on line for food. And like I said, there was nobody to tell me to go or don't go.

speaker: interviewer
text: In this Feldafing, were there soldiers present? American?

speaker: interviewee
text: American, yeah. They used to come around.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they guards? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no. But a lot of-- mostly Jewish. What was happening was, see, they came around and a lot of them spoke Jewish. And people had relatives in America. And they would take the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Addresses?

speaker: interviewee
text: If they knew. But you see, some people just-- they talk, but they never followed through. See, we had an incident that we had met-- my husband happened to remember he had an aunt in the Bronx, and he knew the address by heart. And he would tell  it  to the soldiers. Oh yeah, we'll write. We'll let them know that you're alive, you know. But nothing materialized.

speaker: interviewer
text: One time, there was a jeep drove in with an older man. And he spoke English with a German accent. So I said to him, we weren't married yet. I mean, we were going. I said, why don't you go over to him? He says, leave me alone. I'm getting tired of this whole thing. They all promise, and nothing's happening. I says, come on, what have you got to lose? So he went over, and this man lived in Brooklyn. He went over to him and he told him that he's got an aunt that lives in the Bronx at 1384 Grand Concourse, and her name is so and so.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what he used to do-- see he wasn't-- he got out of Germany in 1939. He just beat it. He wasn't-- he was too old to be in the service. He worked in the censorship, mail censorship. He wore a uniform, American uniform. And he would write to  his wife, and he would follow up. And she would call the people. And this aunt of my husband's got a phone call. And this woman says to her, are you Mrs. [NON-ENGLISH]. She says yes. She says, Marian-- I must have spoken with such a think German accent. She says, I got some news for you. She says to her, you better sit down. She says, your nephew is alive. She couldn't-- She couldn't talk. She gave the phone to her husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's how they found out that, you know. And that's how they sponsored us.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. When did you marry, Phyllis?

speaker: interviewee
text: December 31, 1945. I would have been celebrating my 50th this year.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And his last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Copper.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the European? And what was the American?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cooper.

speaker: interviewer
text: Cooper. All right. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] When did you leave Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: I arrived in the United States September 16, 1946, and we had ten days. I would say, maybe on the 10th of--

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And did you work in Bad Wiessee all that time until you left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not all the time. Because once they sponsored us, we were getting a little too busy, you know, with interviews because they were checking us out.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: The American. So I only worked about six months for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you settle when you came to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Bronx.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you and your husband do to earn a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to work in  a bakery, in a neighborhood bakery. Because that's the only way I could communicate, because it was a Jewish neighborhood. So I spoke Yiddish. My husband's a barber, and he had to have a license. And he couldn't get a license unless he went to school for six months. Which he really didn't need, but that's the law. So I don't know, he worked with somebody. A relative took him in to making baskets or something like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just to earn enough to-- we lived in a furnished room, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, when? In the following years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many children did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: The older one is Marvin, and the younger one [? is Stephen. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Stephen? ?] OK. Well, let's talk about the time that you were settling. What were some of the challenges that you encountered in your attempt to come back into society, into American society?

speaker: interviewee
text: Language.

speaker: interviewer
text: Big barrier?

speaker: interviewee
text: I made-- I made up my mind. And I-- that's another guilt. I made up my mind that I'm gonna learn how to speak English. I was 23 years old when I came to America. I says, this is my country now. I'm going to be  living here. And in the process I-- I tried talking English most of the time, because that's the only way to learn. I even told people, correct me. Keep correcting me. That I-- my kids don't speak Yiddish. And it's bothering me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What affects do you think the horror of the Holocaust had on your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bad. Oh, can I tell you an incident? I don't know if it has any bearing on my personal condition. Before we came to America, we had to go to the German consulate. There were three consuls sort of that they were processing. They were processing the people. And they weren't too sympathetic, believe me, or too nice to us-- some of them. Well, one was miserable. And everybody prayed, God, don't let me get that guy. And one of the men, he was called-- and this was his interviewer. And he had to sign some papers. And he got him so nervous, that man couldn't sign his name. That's how his hand was shaking. Eventually, he got to America. That was no reason not. But at that time, he didn't pass him. This stuck in my mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I came to the United States. At that point, I didn't have any problems. But when I came to America, like I said, I wanted to Americanize myself. I don't know whether it was the right-- was I right or wrong? I wanted, I felt I'm still young enough to. So then I became, we applied for the citizenship papers. And then five years later, we were citizens. And I noticed that as time went on, and I needed to go to some kind of a official place, government authority official, my signature, I'm shaking. Not, not very badly, but I started noticing. And it was getting worse.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I started with my health, you see. I thought I was having cancer of the stomach. I thought I was getting a heart attack because I was getting palpitations. And the doctor, my doctor, got to a point where he said to me-- he, you know, tested me the way they did in those days. He says, there's nothing wrong with you. He says, I think you should see a psychiatrist.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he aware that you were a Holocaust survivor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Yeah, of course. He says, I think you should see a psychiatrist. He sent me to a hospital, and the chief of psychiatry was a Jew. And of course, he heard me talking, you  know. How well did I speak after being in the country, I don't know, five, six years, or whatever. He says, to me, were you in-- are you a Holocaust survivor? I says, yes. He put me on top of the list, because they had a tremendously long wait.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he able to help you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you talk about your experiences, your Shoah experiences, with other people when you first came to this country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me why not?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why not? Because my biggest guilt is that I wasn't in a death camp. I never saw these atrocities till after the liberation. [SOBBING]. So what have I got to tell you when the other people went through what they did? Do you understand? I felt that mine was nothing. And that's what I'm living with.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever discuss this with your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't talk to them too much about it either.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now that it's 50 years since the war ended, what message or what lesson do you have for your children or grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: I hope that nobody ever forgets. And it's even difficult for me to say this, because I did not see any of this. What they show on television, or the and all. I mean, I know it happened, I've seen-- I saw things. I saw people being killed. I saw people starved to death. I saw beatings. I mean, I saw, but I didn't see what I showed you here in the book. I didn't see it. And this is what's killing me. Because I felt that I really don't have a story to tell.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have been telling me a story.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but you see what my grace was. Is this the right way that I'm saying it? I think the fact that they took me away as early as they did. That was my-- my fate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else that you would like to add to your testimony? [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Do you feel you've completed what you want to pass on?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I'm trying. I'm trying to be a decent human being. I tell you something, I went down here yesterday to the pool, and I was terribly upset. They have music twice a month poolside, and the people can dance. I felt that this should have been called off yesterday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because of the current events in Israel, the assassination of the premier?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, some people thought that I was totally wrong.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, Phyllis. Let's just bring your story, your history, up-to-date. How long did [NON-ENGLISH] live?

speaker: interviewee
text: 65, He died at 65.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was that?

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you remarry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I remarried.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your husband's name now?

speaker: interviewee
text: George Young.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Phyllis, I want to thank you so very much for sharing this experience of these terrible years.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't even feel that I like belong on this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you do. And your story is here for history and for posterity. But before we close, we do have some photographs and artifacts that we are going to include on this tape. And again, I do thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Can I say that she was killed?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Who is in this photograph, Phyllis?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister on the left. Her name is, was, [NON-ENGLISH] Kellman. She's holding her baby who was probably around six months at the time. Her name is [NON-ENGLISH]. And the person on the right was a very dear friend of hers. And she's wearing the armband with the star of David, which can be seen the way she's holding, helping my sister hold the baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is [NON-ENGLISH]. And I understand-- after the war, I found out that  she had survived, and she went to Israel. My sister and the baby did not survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: When do you think this photograph was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say probably around the end of 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you happen to have this in your possession?

speaker: interviewee
text: I must have had it on me, because I don't remember having it mailed to me. And I had it with me all the time, all the years I was in camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: As a matter of fact, I had it over my bed, bunk. And I had knocked it in with a nail. And if you look closely, I had the original. This is not the original. I had it enlarged. And the original has the hole in it from the nail.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you can see the photograph.

speaker: interviewee
text: But you could see it on the photograph. Because the people who did it for me, the enlargements, she says, we can take this out. I say, do not take it out.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just leave it the way it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. What are we looking at here, Phyllis?

speaker: interviewee
text: You're looking at a postcard that my sister had written to me. And we where able to get mail at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd say beginning of 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was your sister at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister was still in Sosnowiec. She was still in my home town.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was there until the [NON-ENGLISH], till they uh--

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that? When did that--

speaker: interviewee
text: 1943 They liquidated the ghetto. And that's when she went to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. This photograph, what is this document?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is that hotel that they requisitioned for us. Because this-- this personnel, I told you, they were all Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was Bad Wiessee. And he wanted a few people to work. It was a cushiony little job in a beautiful place. As a matter of fact, you know, like, they smoked. Everybody smoked those days, and when they were watching a movie. And the soldiers would take a puff and throw it down. And he, that lieutenant, he says, goddammit, he says, I don't care what you do with it. But he says, if you're going to throw it down, squash it. I don't want the Germans to pick it up and smoke it after you. Because they would kill for cigarettes in those days.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is where you worked.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is where we worked for about six months. And this is where I got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Bad Wiessee?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: In this building?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, yeah, I think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: If you want to see my wedding album.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What is this little album?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a little album of pictures that were taken at the liberation and--

speaker: interviewer
text: From where?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe-- I believe it might have been the first picture, pictures taken of the camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which camp is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dachau. The camp is Dachau. And also it was published by [NON-ENGLISH]. And it's not just the camps. It goes on to the Nuremberg trial. And the description of the pictures is in four languages.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: French, Polish, English, and I don't know what else.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Tell me about this photograph.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is myself and my late husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: His name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Jerry Cooper.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken, do you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would said probably in the late '60s or maybe early '70s. I'm not sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I really can't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my son Stephen and his wife Lori.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were married in 1981.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my son Marvin and his wife Karen. And they were married in 1983.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Are we on?

speaker: interviewee
text: These are my granddaughters. On the left is Jamie, on the right is Ally. And they are children of [? Stephen ?] and Lori.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And their last name is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cooper.

speaker: interviewer
text: And these young men?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left is Michael. On the right is Robert. And they are sons or Marvin. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Christopher, and he's the son of Marvin and Karen.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] OK, Phyllis.

speaker: interviewee
text: Are you going to take all the people?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, just tell us who, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, on the left is George Young. And I'm next to him, and we have been married now nine years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it looks like you were on  a--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were on a cruise. And this is Mexico, by the way. We are in Cozumel. I think Cozumel or Playa del Carmen, one of those two.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Thank you.
